formulation: Your art depended on one listener — a tyrant held captive by his own curiosity, night after night, slowly changed by a story that never quite ended. The panelists described an audience shattered into millions, each fed a different stream of facts by an algorithm that rewards only black-and-white positions and never needs to hear how the story ends. When the listener is no longer one sovereign who can be transformed but a feed that mistakes every ending for a beginning, does the storyteller still have leverage — or is the algorithm itself a rival story-form so total that only a counter-narrative, not a regulation, could answer it?
context_narrative: In a panel on social media and democracy, Manfred Weber declared with intensity that algorithms have destroyed the shared fact base on which democratic compromise depends — only polarised positions survive, and compromise is made invisible. Katrin Bennhold pressed that Europe's landmark regulations haven't gone far enough; Daiana Fernández Molero countered that premature regulation can backfire as badly as Argentine price controls. Meanwhile, a separate session on transatlantic free speech couldn't even agree on what the disagreement was about — civilisational values, corporate power, or political exclusion — leaving the question of who governs democratic speech profoundly unsettled.

selected_quotes.quote: The biggest threat to future democracies is that social media has destroyed the shared fact base required for democratic discourse and has made compromise — the core principle of democracy — unrewarded and invisible.

The way the current US administration defines freedom of speech is really about the rights of American technology companies, not about conservative values; a genuine conservative or nationalist should not accept foreign tech platforms addressing their citizens with AI-driven content.

Western societies should stop labeling ideological opponents as fascists or Nazis; this practice is dividing society and making constructive dialogue impossible, when what is needed is learning to listen to each other again.

Regulation can be harmful if done poorly or prematurely — Argentina's experience with housing price controls that backfired shows the danger, and attempting to regulate AI without understanding it could be similarly damaging.

rationale: The formulation lands in Scheherazade's core activation territory — the listener relationship, speech as leverage, the withheld ending as political tool — while pushing hard into her stretch zone by asking whether her method survives when the listener is an algorithmic feed rather than a single tyrant, using Weber's diagnosis of the shattered fact base to reframe the regulatory impasse as a contest between rival narrative forms.
